title: Conjecture 1.1 (Alon and Wei)
statement_text: Every $d$-regular graph $G$ on $n$ vertices contains a spanning subgraph $H$ such that $$\left|m(H, k) - \frac{n}{d+1}\right| \leq 2$$ for all $0 \leq k \leq d$.
context_text: Alon and Wei introduced the study of spanning subgraphs of $d$-regular graphs whose degree sequence is as uniformly distributed as possible. This stronger conjecture asks that each degree-$k$ class has size within an absolute additive constant of the ideal value $n/(d+1)$. Both this conjecture and the weaker asymptotic Conjecture 1.2 remain open.
paper_contribution: The paper does not resolve Conjecture 1.1; it confirms the weaker asymptotic version (Conjecture 1.2) for $d = o(n/(\log n)^{12})$ via the irregular random subgraph model.

title: Conjecture 1.2 (Alon and Wei)
statement_text: Let $d = o(n)$. Then every $d$-regular graph $G$ on $n$ vertices contains a spanning subgraph $H$ such that $$m(H, k) = (1 + o(1))\frac{n}{d+1}$$ for all $0 \leq k \leq d$.
context_text: This is the central conjecture of the paper, a weaker asymptotic version of Conjecture 1.1. Prior work of Frieze, Gould, Karoński and Pfender confirmed it for $d \leq (n/\log n)^{1/4}$, and Alon and Wei extended this to $d = o((n/\log n)^{1/3})$ using a more careful concentration argument.
paper_contribution: Confirms Conjecture 1.2 for all $d = o(n/(\log n)^{12})$ by proving (Theorem 1.3) that the irregular random subgraph $H = H(G)$ satisfies $m(H,k) = (1+o(1))n/(d+1)$ for all $0 \leq k \leq d$ with high probability, via a martingale Bernstein inequality argument.

title: Informal Conjecture on Property (*) for d = o(n/log n)
statement_text: The irregular random subgraph $H = H(G)$ satisfies Property $(*)$ — that is, $m(H,k) = (1+o(1))n/(d+1)$ for all $0 \leq k \leq d$ — with high probability for all $d = o(n/\log n)$.
context_text: After proving Theorem 1.3 for $d = o(n/(\log n)^{12})$, the authors conjecture the range extends to $d = o(n/\log n)$. They also remark that for $d = \omega(n/\log n)$ the random subgraph model likely fails to satisfy Property $(*)$, and that new ideas would be required to establish Conjecture 1.2 in the full range.
notes: Stated in prose without a labelled theorem environment: 'We conjecture that with high probability for all d = o(n/log n), the irregular random subgraph H satisfies Property *'.
